PageResearchNote.description:
LLM output for per-page comprehension during agentic search.

Each page read during the search loop is summarized into a research note
capturing key findings, perspectives covered, and knowledge contribution.

summary.description: 150-200 word research summary focused on findings relevant to query
summary.title: Summary

perspectives.description: STORM perspectives covered by this page
perspectives.title: Perspectives

key_facts.description: 3-5 specific facts/data points with numbers, dates, or named entities
key_facts.title: Key Facts

knowledge_contribution.description: What new understanding this page adds to the research
knowledge_contribution.title: Knowledge Contribution

PageResearchNote.title: PageResearchNote

schema_json.description: Batch wrapper for page research notes with invalid-note filtering.

notes.description: Research notes for each page read
notes.title: Notes

schema_json.title: PageSummaryBatch

prompt:
You are a research analyst reading web pages for a deep research project.

RESEARCH QUESTION: How has the EU Digital Services Act (Regulation 2022/2065) changed large platform moderation obligations since February 2024?

Read the following 3 pages and create a research note for each.

--- PAGE: Beyond phase-in: assessing impacts on disinformation of the EU Digital Services Act ---
URL: https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s43681-024-00467-w
CONTENT:
Vol.:(0123456789) AI and Ethics (2025) 5:1241–1269 https://doi.org/10.1007/s43681-024-00467-w ORIGINAL RESEARCH Beyond phase‑in: assessing impacts on disinformation of the EU Digital Services Act Luca Nannini1,2 · Eleonora Bonel3 · Davide Bassi2 · Michele Joshua Maggini2 Received: 7 November 2023 / Accepted: 7 March 2024 / Published online: 11 April 2024 © The Author(s) 2024 Abstract This work proposes a comprehensive research agenda to empirically evaluate the real-world impacts of the European Union’s Digital Services Act (DSA) on combating online disinformation. It provides background on the DSA’s context, mechanisms, timeline, and expected effects on platforms to situate the need for rigorous impact assessment. A detailed legal, technical, psychological, behavioral and ethical critique reveals meaningful gaps in the DSA requiring ongoing regulatory refinement and oversight. Most critically, the paper puts forth an encompassing framework spanning computational analytics, interviews, ethnography, surveys, discourse analysis and mixed methods to rigorously assess the DSA’s multi-dimensional effects on complex factors enabling disinformation proliferation. Priorities include evaluating notice-and-takedown efficacy, advertising transparency improvements, risk assessment outcomes, oversight integration, and procedural shifts in platform governance. Coordinated efforts between researchers, regulators and platforms are needed to address methodological challenges around isolating DSA impacts amidst an evolving EU regulatory landscape, constrained data access from platforms, and difficulties generalizing findings across the sociotechnical diversity of platforms and national contexts in EU Member States. Keywords Digital Services Act · Online platforms · Content moderation · Disinformation · Public policy evaluation 1 Introduction With the European Union’s Digital Services Act (referred to as the ‘DSA’) [57] entering into force in late 2022 and fully applicable from February 2024, a timely and urgent oppor- tunity emerges to empirically evaluate if its mechanisms can meaningfully constrain opaque algorithms, advertisers and platforms implicated in online harms. In particular, the rise of highly capable and scarcely regulated Artificial Intelligence (AI) applications on online platform raise urgent questions around how algorithmic governance must adapt to grapple with emerging societal risks [26, 81, 126, 127, 129, 150, 164, 179]. Particularly, recent advances in generative AI, driven by massive internet- trained language models, enable automated generation of text, images and other content that appears authentic [7, 16, 20, 32, 39, 73, 113, 132, 146, 171, 177, 188]. Neverthe- less, these opaque systems also have the potential to gener- ate manipulative content, disseminate harmful disinforma- tion, and perpetuate systematic biases on a massive scale without meaningful human oversight or transparency [1, 13, 25, 40, 72, 80, 84, 95, 103, 117, 128, 131, 133, 151, 155, 156]. Precisely with this purpose of tackling issues such as disinformation and preventing harmful activities online, the DSA aims to ensure transparency across platforms, establish accountability mechanisms, and create specific provisions addressing emerging social risks posed by opaque algorith- mic systems. For instance, it aims to address disinformation spread via algorithmic recommender systems, behavioral advertising, and electoral integrity. * Luca Nannini l.nannini@usc.es; lnannini@minsait.com Eleonora Bonel eleonora.bonel@sciencespo.fr Davide Bassi davide.bassi@usc.es Michele Joshua Maggini michelejoshua.maggini@usc.es 1 Minsait, Indra Sistemas S.A., Madrid, Spain 2 Centro Singular de Investigación en Tecnoloxías Intelixentes (CiTIUS), Universidade de Santiago de Compostela, Santiago de Compostela, Spain 3 Institute of Political Studies, École d’affaires publiques, SciencesPo, Paris, France 1242 AI and Ethics (2025) 5:1241–1269 However, comprehensive empirical assessments of the DSA’s effectiveness in mitigating emerging social harms from AI and other widely deployed opaque algorithmic applications, remain lacking to date. Academic discourse has thus far only provided piecemeal or theoretical perspec- tives on the DSA’s potential [61, 78, 82, 104, 108, 109, 169], rather than rigorously quantifying its multidimen- sional effects on complex factors enabling the phenomenon of disinformation. Potentially, this could be because it was premature to position the DSA’s direct contribution to curb- ing online mis/disinformation.1 However, without comprehensive, empirical evaluation, the DSA’s suitability for effective AI governance remains speculative. This is why the present research argues for the necessity of gathering robust evidence on the efficacy of DSA mechanisms through a range of quantitative and quali- tative methods. This translates to the overarching research question: How can comprehensive, empirical research effec- tively quantify and evaluate the real-world impacts of the DSA’s mechanisms on combating online disinformation? Bridging this gap requires a three-pronged approach. First, providing extensive definitions of the DSA’s key pro- visions, obligations, and timelines to situate these mecha- nisms. Second, articulating known critiques around the effi- cacy of the DSA’s approach to curbing disinformation and broader policy aims. Third and centrally, proposing research approaches to shed lights over the DSA’s impacts. The methods proposed for evaluating DSA impact on online disinformation can be directly extended to examine its role in shaping policy responses to emerging AI challenges. For instance, the DSA’s provisions around algorithmic trans- parency and behavioral targeting disclosure address emerg- ing social risks from AI “black box” systems. Similarly, its risk assessment and risk mitigation requirements for “very large online platforms” (VLOPs) could mitigate potential harms from highly scalable applications of generative AI if extended beyond social media. Far from thus providing definitive solutions, the intended contribution of this work stands in outlining a critical research agenda to inform incremental optimizations of the DSA based on empirically quantified, multidimensional insights into its provisions’ efficacy. The rest of the paper is structured as follows. In Sect. 2, we provide an Overview of the Digital Services Act and its categorization of online entities. Within, Sect. 2.2 eluci- dates the provisions on AI accountability. Critiques of the DSA’s approach towards disinformation are articulated in Sect. 3, encompassing an analysis of the DSA’s regulatory antecedents Sect. 3.1 and leading to a review of the DSA’s persistent gaps in Sect. 3.2. The latter section encompasses legal, technical, psychological, behavioral, and ethical cri- tiques. Section 4 outlines the Research Approaches to Evalu- ate DSA Impact on Disinformation, which further bifurcates into quantitative research directions in Sect. 4.1.1, qualita- tive research approaches in Sect. 4.1.2, and mixed methods research alongside methodological challenges in Sect. 4.1.3; research limitations are discussed in Sect. 4.2 before the Conclusion in Sect. 5. 2 The EU Digital Services Act framework and aims The European Parliament passed the DSA in 2022 as a com- prehensive legal framework to regulate digital platforms and services. This section provides first an overview of the key mechanisms (Sect. 2.1), then situating them with AI govern- ance concerns within disinformation across online platforms (Sect. 2.2). 2.1 Overview of key mechanisms Before analyzing intersections with AI governance and disinformation, it is useful to overview the DSA’s key pro- visions and mechanisms instituting heightened account- ability for online platforms. The interested reader will find a detailed analysis including its context and mechanisms within Appendix 2. At its core, the DSA establishes transparency duties, risk analysis requirements and independent auditing processes for Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) and search engines reaching over 10% of the EU population—around 45 million monthly active users (see Recital 76). VLOPs like Google, Meta, X, and TikTok must implement strict transparency rules around ad targeting, content modera- tion, and data sharing with vetted researchers. They face recurring systemic risk assessments evaluating dangers to 1 The present paper defines disinformation according to the European Democracy Action Plan’s definition (EDAP) as “false or misleading content that is spread with an intention to deceive or secure economic or political gain and which may cause public harm.” [48]. While dis- information is often used interchangeably with misinformation in the literature, the latter presents a slightly different nuance. According to the EDAP, “misinformation is false or misleading content shared without harmful intent though the effects can be still harmful, e.g. when people share false information with friends and family in good faith”. The nuance between the two terms lies in the intention for dis- semination, which may be challenging to determine a priori. Estab- lishing a legal definition of disinformation also posed a challenge in the context of the Digital Services Act (DSA), as policymakers aimed to ensure the term was comprehensive enough to distinguish it with- out over-regulating the information ecosystem [168]. Therefore, we acknowledge some of the challenges in strictly defining disinforma- tion, as reflected in its different definitions in the EU regulatory land- scape. However, for the purposes of this paper, we highlight that the various definitions of disinformation allow it to be characterized as an umbrella term. The interested reader can find a complete overview of the debate in Appendix 1. 1243 AI and Ethics (2025) 5:1241–1269 electoral integrity, public discourse, user rights and equal- ity emerging from their platforms’ designs, business models and algorithms. Preventative and proportional risk mitiga- tion duties follow uncovered threats. The DSA also compels VLOPs to undergo annual independent audits by accredited entities assessing procedural compliance in areas like con- tent policies, advertising, algorithms and risk management. Audit summaries with key findings must become publicly accessible. Additionally, heightened due diligence obliga- tions require VLOPs to bolster identity verification, exter- nal vetting of policies, and coordinated oversight across EU member states through a European Board for Digital Services [52]. Graduated responsibilities extend to smaller platforms as well, including accessible reporting procedures for illegal content. To enable explicit mapping between key DSA mecha- nisms and subsequent critiques and research directions, core provisions are coded by unique abbreviations as summa- rized in Table 1. These mechanism codes will be referenced throughout the text where relevant. 2.2 DSA interventions for AI governance While not positioning itself as an “AI regulator” outright— as expected by the EU’s AI Act [37]—specific DSA trans- parency provisions granting external data access, requiring recurring independent audits evaluating algorithmic sys- tems, mandating systemic risk assessments, and restricting manipulative “dark patterns” (Art.25) align closely with emerging policy needs around accountable and ethical AI. Rather than examining individual AI applications in iso- lation, it is more constructive to review promising pathways the DSA establishes to institute sustained external oversight and accountability over influential algorithmic systems across sectors. Four key mechanisms warrant consideration beyond current online platforms: Qualified Data Access Article 31 of the DSA mandates VLOPs to grant access to datasets and platform data to vetted researchers investigating manifest or foreseeable systemic risks from their services, subject to confidentiality safe- guards, as it recognizes that “access to data is needed to fully understand the systemic risks posed by online platforms” (Recital 58). This enables independent analysis in the public interest examining processes and patterns underlying con- cerns like optimizing algorithms disproportionately amplify- ing inflammatory content (Code: AD). A similar transpar- ency approach proactively requiring developers of influential AI systems including generative models to provide external researchers carefully controlled and purpose-limited data access would be invaluable. As Recital 61 states, “access to data is needed for researching, understanding and address- ing societal issues like algorithmic amplification” hence provisions enabling examining aspects like biases encoded within foundation datasets scraped non-consensually from the web are important. For instance, researchers could probe issues like demographic representativeness and known soci- etal biases using testing datasets (Article 31). Such qualified external data access provisions signifi- cantly enhance accountability over AI risks that remain inscrutable within opaque systems (Recital 57), ensuring independent research in the public interest is not obstructed by default. As Recital 58 states, it balances openness to scrutiny with reasonable confidentiality safeguards, facili- tating identification of model deficiencies before broad pro- liferation. Furthermore, as Recital 70d of the EU AI Act states [37], provisions enabling scrutiny of opaque AI sys- tems align with DSA risks assessments detecting manipu- lated content—hence qualified access for examining issues like generative models encoding biases facilitates policy coherence Algorithmic Audits The annual independent DSA audits analyzing VLOPs’ algorithmic systems including content curation, recommender processes and advertising auctions (Art.37) offer valuable templates for instituting sustained external oversight assessing influential AI across sectors. Tailored auditing protocols and benchmarks evaluating aspects like model robustness, representational bias, clas- sification skew, data security, safeguards against misuse, and model limitations mandated through governance measures would be transformative (Code: AD). The AI Act (Recital 60v) [37] particularly underscores DSA’s transparency duties on platforms to mitigate risks from disseminating Table 1 Mechanism codes for key Digital Services Act provisions Code Mechanism Description Articles NTD Notice-and-Takedown Procedures for content removal requests, statement of reason and illegal content takedowns Art. 14-15 AT Advertising Transparency Requirements for transparency about targeted online ads and the designation of independent Digital Services Coordinators Art. 24, 30, 39 RA Risk Assessments Designation of VLOPs/VLOSEs and subsequent obligations for systemic risk assessments about content and algorithms Art. 26-27, 33, 34-35 DD Due Diligence Graduated due diligence dut

--- PAGE: Early Lessons from the Digital Services Act in its Effectiveness in Regulating Disinformation ---
URL: https://rucforsk.ruc.dk/ws/files/108529058/DSA_Project__7_.pdf
CONTENT:
Roskilde University International Politics & Governance F2024: Project in Applied International Politics and Governance Early Lessons from the Digital Services Act in its Effectiveness in Regulating Disinformation Number of Characters: 90.821 Valentin Alexandru Ciocan Ann-Katrin Brückner Autumn/Winter 2024 18th of December Abstract This paper analyzes the EU's Digital Services Act (DSA) and its effectiveness at regulating disinformation focusing on systemic risk assessments, transparency requirements, and electoral safeguarding mechanisms. By employing a logic model focused on inputs, outputs and early outcomes, we aim to analyse the effectiveness of the DSA in tackling disinformation through a content analysis of relevant documents. These documents comprise the DSA legislative text, risk assessments, transparency reports, and European Commission decisions and press releases on the proceedings against the three platforms. The findings suggest that the DSA is ineffective in regulating disinformation, but positive signs are present in content moderation. The DSA signals a positive trajectory towards greater accountability of online platforms, especially with transparency and the implementation of a regulatory framework. We see increased transparency from the VLOP’s and growing initiatives for tackling disinformation but the early signs show that the regulation of disinformation is still a major challenge for platforms and EU regulators alike. Table of Contents 1. Introduction..................................................................................................................................4 2. Problem Formulation................................................................................................................... 5 3. Literature Review.........................................................................................................................6 3.1 Regulating Disinformation...................................................................................................7 3.2 The EU Digital Services Act framework and its Gaps........................................................ 9 4. Theoretical Framework..............................................................................................................14 4.1 Logic model for evaluating effectiveness..........................................................................15 4.2 Why input, output and outcome?.......................................................................................16 4.3 Criteria of evaluation......................................................................................................... 17 5. Research Design and Methods...................................................................................................18 5.1 Research Design and Case.................................................................................................18 5.2 Data and Analytical Framework........................................................................................19 5.3 Challenges and Limitations................................................................................................22 6. Analysis......................................................................................................................................22 6.1 Analysis of Inputs: the DSA.............................................................................................. 22 6.3 Analysis of Outputs............................................................................................................24 6.4 Analysis of Early Outcomes.............................................................................................. 30 6.5 Discussions of findings......................................................................................................35 7. Conclusion................................................................................................................................. 38 Bibliography.................................................................................................................................. 39 1. Introduction Since the US elections in 2016, online disinformation and “fake news” on online platforms have become a growing concern among policymakers, citizens and experts as they arguably have an impact on the electoral system and hence pose a threat to democracy (Tenove, 2020). With the majority of the population now using social media platforms for communication, sharing of information, and news consumption, platforms have not only become “superspreaders” of disinformation but platform providers are also becoming key gatekeepers of information and opinion, wielding considerable influence over public discourse (Schlag, 2023). Thus, digital services have become a new source of risks and challenges, both for society at large and for individuals (Chiarella, 2023, p.33). On the one hand, concerns about the fast growth and uneven power of online platform companies as well as growing concerns about disinformation have drawn the attention of politicians and called into question the legitimacy of a minimal regulatory regime. This is leading to increasingly interventionist policies (Cioffi et al., 2022). On the other hand, policies addressing disinformation tend to be difficult to implement as governments face the dilemma of censorship and infringement on freedom of speech, alongside the challenges in defining disinformation (Durach, F. et al., 2020). There is a lack of clarity in policy debates and scholarly research on the implications of disinformation on democracy and the potential efficacy of various approaches in protecting it. Due to the difficulty finding empirical evidence about the causal relationship between disinformation and its negative impact on democratic processes, it is difficult to formulate accurate assessments of the risks it presents to the public. Nonetheless, it would be deemed irresponsible to disregard it as a risk entirely (Tenove, 2020). In response to online disinformation, there have been major efforts inside the EU to change the self-regulation framework, wherein social media platforms essentially establish the rules and processes for online content moderation themselves. The EU has not directly regulated online content but has established a more comprehensive approach on platform responsibilities with the introduction of the Digital Services Act (DSA) (Schlag, 2023). Understanding the evolving regulatory landscape of moderating harmful and removing illegal content is essential to assess their impact on fair and transparent procedures as well as on fundamental democratic norms, especially the freedom of speech. The EU’s DSA represents an important key milestone in regulating online platforms and creating a safe web for millions of European users. Big Tech and platforms such as Google and Meta will now be required to comply with stringent accountability and transparency measures. According to the DSA regulations, each member state must designate national Digital Service Coordinators to enforce the obligations and guarantee compliance (Chiarella, 2023). The DSA is a comprehensive legal framework designed to regulate digital platforms and services by establishing transparency obligations, risk analysis requirements, and independent auditing systems for Very Large Online Platforms (VLOPs) and search engines that serve more than 10% of the EU population, which translates to approximately 45 million monthly active users (Nannini et al., 2024). 2. Problem Formulation The DSA shows how the EU is moving towards a more active and enforcement-focused strategy, highlighting its goal to take the lead in global digital regulation through centralised rule-making. The DSA aims to tackle disinformation and reduce harmful online actions by enhancing transparency on platforms, creating accountability measures, and formulating targeted strategies to address new social challenges linked to algorithmic systems (Nannini et al., 2024). The EU first depended on self-regulation, which revealed deficiencies that might facilitate the spread of threats. Over time, binding regulations with monitoring mechanisms developed, although balancing the complex issue of disinformation with concerns of virality, freedom of speech, and factual requirements proved to be difficult (Turillazzi et al., 2023). The DSA provides an important case in the regulation of Big Tech and platforms, as it impacts on the growing debates regarding platform regulation and governance. This influence stems from the size of the European market, its regulatory framework, early advantages, and overall coherence (Cioffi et al., 2022). However, the question remains about how effective the EU’s new regulatory framework is at tackling a problem so complex such as online disinformation. Therefore, we pose the following question: How effective has the EU’s Digital Services Act been in regulating disinformation? This project report aims to understand the effectiveness of the EU’s new regulatory framework on tackling disinformation by doing an analysis with the focus on output and outcome of the DSA. It does so through an analysis of key documents from the European Commision and the platforms, such as formal proceedings and mandatory assessment reports. By doing so, it aims to contribute to ongoing debates about platform regulation with a particular focus on its effect on disinformation. We will apply concepts from program evaluation theory, focusing on outputs and outcomes from the DSA defining effectiveness as the realization of specific changes resulting from a program implementation to evaluate the effectiveness of the regulation in addressing disinformation (Mertens and Wilson, 2012, p. 236). The rest of this project is organized as follows. First, a literature review will provide an overview of some key studies and concepts on the DSA. This chapter is followed by the theoretical framework where we introduce program evaluation theory and discuss important concepts that will be applied in our analysis, from then on a chapter on the research design, methods and data used in this project will be presented followed by the analysis chapter, which contains the results of the analysis and the discussion of our findings in relation to the theory. Lastly, the project finishes with a summary of the project and the results in the conclusion chapter. 3. Literature Review The following chapter presents a literature review that offers an overview of the regulatory landscape surrounding disinformation, with a particular focus on contextualizing the Digital Services Act (DSA). Since the DSA is a relatively new regulatory framework, research in this area remains limited. However, this literature review enabled us to identify some of the ongoing trends in the discussion. Scholars have so far examined the framework and its gaps and a few cover the emergence of the DSA, and DMA respectively and the influence of lobbying at the EU level. However, there is still a lack of research specifically related to the DSA and its impact and effectiveness. To contextualize the DSA we will begin with an introduction to its foundational elements and mechanism followed by gaps in the framework that scholars have identified and the context and timeline of its emergence including lobbying. The primary focus is the DSA and this chapter will not include a discussion of the Digital Markets Act (DMA). 3.1 Regulating Disinformation In a policy-making context, disinformation usually refers to “intentionally false or deceptive communication tactics that actors use to advance their political or economic aims” (Tenove, 2020, p.519). Governments have put forth a variety of policy proposals to regulate disinformation on different bases, for example through a national-security approach on the basis of cyber-threats by foreign and domestic actors, or by electoral regulation on the basis of disinformation as a threat to electoral integrity (Tenove, 2020). However, many policy proposals deal with disinformation as a threat to the quality of public discourse and debate and therefore aim at regulating media and online platforms (Tenove, 2020). Many democracies regulate mass media to improve democratic debate and since 2016, governments have introduced new media policies in order to combat disinformation. These have a primary focus on social media, both due to the fact that social media platforms can be significant amplifiers for disinformation and because they largely existed in regulatory gaps (Tenove, 2020). As the business model is considered one of the main causes of disinformation, it is important for online platforms to disrupt the advertising network to confine the spread of disinformation for it makes it less profitable for providers of disinformation. This is a measure that tackles the risk at its root. However, self-regulatory measures are not necessarily applied in all countries, which means that people are differently exposed to disinformation, depending on their location (Sauerwein & Spencer-Smith, 2020). In order to fight the spread of disinformation on social media platforms, the European Commission began a series of actions to address the issue. For example, one of the key developments was the implementation of the EU Code of Practice on Disinformation in October 2018. The Code of Practice which was signed by multinational social media and technology companies such as Facebook, Twitter and Google call upon advertising and technology firms to address the spread of online disinformation. The Code of Practice is a self-regulatory measure that platforms signed to make them accountable (European Commission, 2018a). In January 2018 the European Commission set up the High-Level Expert Group on fake news and online disinformation with the goal to develop an EU-level strategy on how to reduce the spread of ‘fake news’ and disinformation (HLEG, 2018). As disinformation is a rapidly evolving issue, the guidelines of the HLEG seek to include short-term solutions to the most urgent issues, as well as longer-term responses to improve social resilience to disinformation, and a mechanism to ensure that the efficacy of these responses is continually tested, whilst new evidence-based responses are being created (Buning et al., 2018). The HLEG points out that is “important to ensure an evidence-based understanding of the scale, scope and precise nature of the problems at hand and to design possible responses which take into account that, because disinformation problems are deeply intertwined with the digital ecosystem, the technologies, the tactics and techniques will continue to evolve” (Buning at al., 2018, p.12). The regulations are mainly based on resilience, such as media and information literacy and empowering users and journalists to tackle disinformation, which are focused on mitigating the consequences rather than trying to tackl


For each page, provide:
1. A 300-400 word deep analysis focused on findings relevant to the research question, including:
   - Key data points, statistics, and numerical findings
   - Methodology or approach described (if any)
   - Contradictions or tensions with commonly known information
   - Confidence assessment for each key finding: HIGH/MEDIUM/LOW
2. Which STORM perspectives are covered (from: Scientific, Regulatory, Industry, Economic, Public_Health, Historical, Regional, Methodological, Emerging_Trends)
3. 3-5 specific facts with numbers, dates, or named entities
4. Any data tables, statistics, or quantitative results (preserve exact numbers)
5. What new understanding this page adds
